summary: Jeff Atwood makes a passionate case for triple-monitor setups, celebrating that ATI's new video cards with three display outputs have finally made this configuration accessible to mainstream users. He walks through the technical hurdles of connecting three monitors, particularly the DisplayPort conversion challenge, while noting the benefits of simplified hardware configuration. The post serves as both a practical guide and advocacy piece, arguing that three monitors hit a 'sweet spot' with a natural center focus and 50% more display area than dual monitors. Atwood dismisses Nvidia's competing GTX 480 as wasteful and endorses ATI cards across all price points for this purpose.
key_points: Triple monitors now achievable with a single ATI video card upgrade, eliminating the need for exotic cards or dual video card setups
Three monitors provides a natural center focus that dual monitors lack, representing the productivity sweet spot before diminishing returns
DisplayPort-to-DVI conversion is the main technical hurdle, requiring either a $30 passive or $110 active adapter
Moving to a single video card simplifies configuration, reduces power consumption, and eliminates driver conflicts
For non-gamers, the cheapest Eyefinity-capable card ($100) is sufficient for all desktop GUI needs
money_quotes: As far as I'm concerned, you can never be too rich, too thin, or have too much screen space.
If you're only using one monitor, you are cheating yourself out of potential productivity.
With three monitors, there's a 'center' to focus on. And 50% more display area.
I don't care how large a single display can be; you can never have enough desktop space.
Basically, it makes triple monitors – dare I say it – almost a mainstream desktop configuration.
It turns out their new GTX 480 fails in other ways, in that it's basically the Pentium 4 of video cards – generating ridiculous amounts of heat for very little performance gain.
key_insight: The technological barrier to triple-monitor productivity has finally fallen, making what was once an exotic setup accessible to anyone willing to navigate a minor DisplayPort conversion challenge.
